paper_summary.title: Transverse-Dominant Anisotropic Dispersion and Transient Trapping in 3D Solenoidal Turbulence
paper_summary.main_objective: The study investigates the relationship between large-scale solenoidal forcing, coherent vortex structures, and passive tracer transport in 3D turbulence, specifically characterizing dispersion anisotropy and the impact of vortex trapping.
paper_summary.main_contribution_summary: The authors identify a persistent transverse-dominant anisotropy in tracer dispersion (λ ≈ 0.52) relative to the large-scale flow and demonstrate that vortex trapping is transient (residing ~7% of a large-eddy turnover time), preventing long-term memory or anomalous transport like Lévy flights.

claims.claim_text: Dispersion perpendicular to the instantaneous local large-scale velocity field systematically exceeds parallel dispersion.
claims.normalized_claim: Transverse-dominant anisotropic dispersion in solenoidal turbulence.

claims.claim_text: Vortex trapping events are highly transient, lasting only about 7% of a large-eddy turnover time.
The short trapping durations are insufficient to generate long-term memory or Lévy flights, resulting in Gaussian or platykurtic displacement distributions.
claims.normalized_claim: Absence of anomalous transport due to transient trapping.

claims.claim_text: Mean-Square Displacement (MSD) transitions through ballistic, superdiffusive, and diffusive regimes before geometric saturation.
Long-time Finite-Time Lyapunov Exponents (FTLE) do not distinguish between trapped and free tracer dynamics.
uncertainties: Long-time FTLE might mask local chaotic stretching relevant for ejection.

claim_relations.justification: Short residence times in vortices (C2) provide the physical mechanism for the lack of long-term memory required for anomalous transport (C3).

display_items.title_or_caption_summary: Visualization of the Q-criterion field showing vortex cores (yellow) and strain regions (purple).
Mean-Square Displacement (MSD) and scaling exponent alpha(t) transitions.

panel_descriptions.description: MSD for ensemble, trapped, and free cohorts against lag time.

display_items.title_or_caption_summary: Dynamic anisotropy ratio lambda(t) showing the transition to transverse dominance.
Lagrangian autocorrelation of the Q-criterion defining the trapping timescale.
Probability Density Functions of displacements at different lag times.
FTLE distributions for trapped vs free cohorts.
Correlation between FTLE and vortex residence (Left) and FTLE at vortex exit (Right).

panel_descriptions.description: Scatter plot of FTLE vs vortex residence fraction.
Histogram of FTLE at the moment of vortex exit.

evidence_spans.quoted_text: For t > 0.5, the anisotropy stabilizes at a mean value of λ ≈ 0.52 ± 0.045. This result, λ < 1, reveals a persistent and significant anisotropy where dispersion perpendicular to the local large-scale velocity systematically exceeds parallel dispersion.
The function exhibits a rapid decay, crossing the 1/e threshold at a characteristic timescale of τQ ≈ 0.200. ... τQ/Te ≈ 0.077.

By an intermediate time of t = 2.0, the displacement PDF is statistically indistinguishable from a Gaussian distribution, as confirmed by a Kolmogorov-Smirnov test (p = 0.53). ... at late times ... they become platykurtic.

The evolution of the MSD reveals four distinct transport regimes... ballistic (α ≈ 2)... superdiffusive (0.3 < t < 1.5)... diffusive (α ≈ 1) for 1.5 < t < 4... At late times (t > 4)... geometric saturation.
The FTLE distributions for the Trapped and Free tracer cohorts are nearly identical, with statistically indistinguishable mean values (-0.008 ± 0.013 for Trapped vs. -0.007 ± 0.013 for Free).
Regions with Q > 0 correspond to areas where the rotation rate dominates the strain rate.

global_uncertainties: The paper uses a sharp spectral filter (n=1-3) to define the large-scale velocity; results for anisotropy ratio λ may be sensitive to the choice of filter cutoff.
Long-time FTLE is used, which the authors admit might be a weak indicator of the local ejection physics compared to short-time FTLE.
